questions.question: Define Optimal Substructure and Overlapping Subproblems in the context of Dynamic Programming. Give one example of a problem that exhibits each property.
keyPoints: Optimal Substructure: optimal solution can be constructed from optimal solutions of subproblems (e.g., Fibonacci, Knapsack)
Overlapping Subproblems: same subproblems are solved multiple times (e.g., Fibonacci recursion tree has repeated nodes)
Fibonacci exhibits both properties
Short but complete definition with examples required

questions.question: Explain the difference between Memoization (Top-Down) and Tabulation (Bottom-Up) approaches in Dynamic Programming. Which one would you prefer for computing C(100,50) and why?
keyPoints: Memoization: recursive, solves subproblems on-demand, stores only when needed
Tabulation: iterative, fills table from base cases upward
For C(100,50): prefer space-optimized tabulation with O(50) space rather than full 2D table
Tabulation avoids recursion stack overhead
Memoization may compute fewer subproblems if not all are needed

questions.question: Compute the 6th Catalan number (C₆) using the recurrence relation. Show all intermediate steps.
keyPoints: Catalan recurrence: Cₙ = Σ Cᵢ × Cₙ₋₁₋ᵢ for i=0 to n-1, C₀=1
C₁ = C₀×C₀ = 1×1 = 1
C₂ = C₀×C₁ + C₁×C₀ = 1×1 + 1×1 = 2
C₃ = C₀×C₂ + C₁×C₁ + C₂×C₀ = 1×2 + 1×1 + 2×1 = 5
C₄ = C₀×C₃ + C₁×C₂ + C₂×C₁ + C₃×C₀ = 1×5 + 1×2 + 2×1 + 5×1 = 14
C₅ = C₀×C₄ + C₁×C₃ + C₂×C₂ + C₃×C₁ + C₄×C₀ = 42
C₆ = sum of products = 132 (verify)

questions.question: Why is the naive recursive Fibonacci algorithm inefficient? Derive its time complexity and explain how Dynamic Programming improves it.
keyPoints: Naive recursion: T(n) = T(n-1) + T(n-2) + O(1), solving to T(n) = O(2^n)
Exponential because same subproblems computed multiple times (e.g., F(n-2) computed exponentially many times)
DP with memoization: each subproblem solved once, T(n) = O(n)
DP with tabulation: same O(n) time, fills table iteratively
Space reduces from O(n) call stack to O(n) array or O(1) with optimization

questions.question: How many ways can you climb a staircase with 10 steps if you can take 1 or 2 steps at a time? Show the recurrence and compute the answer.
keyPoints: Recurrence: ways(n) = ways(n-1) + ways(n-2), with ways(1)=1, ways(2)=2
This is equivalent to F(n+1) where F is Fibonacci with F(0)=0, F(1)=1
Alternate: Use DP to compute iteratively in O(n) time

questions.question: Prove that the problem of counting the number of ways to parenthesize a product of n+1 factors exhibits optimal substructure.
keyPoints: Let P(n) be number of ways to parenthesize n+1 factors
For any parenthesization, the outermost operation splits factors into two groups: first k+1 factors and last n-k factors
Left side has P(k) ways, right side has P(n-k-1) ways
Total: P(n) = Σ P(k) × P(n-k-1) for k=0 to n-1
This is exactly the Catalan recurrence, proving optimal substructure
The optimal solution to P(n) is built from optimal solutions of subproblems

questions.question: Design a dynamic programming algorithm to compute C(50,25). Write the recurrence relation, base cases, and analyze time and space complexity.
keyPoints: Recurrence: C(n,k) = C(n-1,k-1) + C(n-1,k)
Base cases: C(n,0) = 1, C(n,n) = 1
DP table: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1] + dp[i-1][j] for j ≤ i
Time complexity: O(n×k) = O(50×25) = O(1250)
Space: O(k) = O(25) if using rolling array
Avoids overflow using modulo if needed

questions.question: Write a complete algorithm using Dynamic Programming to compute the nth Fibonacci number. Implement both memoized (top-down) and tabulated (bottom-up) versions. Analyze their time and space complexities, and discuss when each approach is preferable.
keyPoints: Memoized version: recursive with memo array, check if computed before proceeding
Tabulated version: iterative filling of dp array from 2 to n
Time complexity: both O(n)
Space: memoization O(n) for array + O(n) recursion stack; tabulation O(n) for array
Space-optimized tabulation: O(1) using two variables
Memoization preferable when not all subproblems need solving (sparse dependencies)
Tabulation preferable when all subproblems must be solved or memory is constrained
Code should be clean, well-commented with proper base cases

questions.question: Explain three fundamental examples of Dynamic Programming: Fibonacci Numbers, Binomial Coefficient, and Catalan Numbers. For each, provide: (i) the problem definition, (ii) recurrence relation, (iii) DP solution approach, (iv) time and space complexity, and (v) one real-world application.
keyPoints: Fibonacci: F(n) = F(n-1) + F(n-2), F(0)=0, F(1)=1. Applications: staircase climbing, spiral arrangements. O(n) time with DP.
Binomial Coefficient: C(n,k) = C(n-1,k-1) + C(n-1,k), C(n,0)=C(n,n)=1. Applications: Pascal's triangle, combinatorial counting. O(n×k) time.
Catalan Numbers: C(n) = Σ C(i)×C(n-1-i), C(0)=1. Applications: BST count, parenthesizations, polygon triangulations. O(n²) time.
All three exhibit optimal substructure and overlapping subproblems
Emphasize DP solution approach for each including tabulation/memoization options

questions.question: A company wants to distribute n different items among two groups such that each group gets an equal number of items (n is even). In how many ways can this be done? Solve using dynamic programming and show that this is related to the binomial coefficient. Derive the recurrence relation and complexity analysis.
keyPoints: Number of ways to choose n/2 items from n items: C(n, n/2)
DP formulation: ways(i,j) = ways(i-1,j-1) + ways(i-1,j)
Base cases: ways(i,0)=ways(i,i)=1 for all i
This IS the binomial coefficient C(n, n/2)
Time: O(n×n/2) = O(n²), Space: O(n/2) with optimization
Related to middle element of Pascal's triangle row n
Example: n=4 → C(4,2)=6 ways; n=6 → C(6,3)=20 ways
